summary.difficultyAssessment: D1:39 (31%), D2:49 (39%), D3:37 (30%) — excellent match to GL target distribution of ~30:40:30. Difficulty calibration is generally good: D1 uses common vocabulary and obvious relationships (puppy/dog, short/tall), D2 uses moderate vocabulary with 1-2 plausible distractors (sculptor/chisel, drizzle/downpour), and D3 uses advanced vocabulary and abstract relationships (flax/linen, archipelago, acquit/innocent). However, several D1 questions use overly generic tip text about degree relationships that has nothing to do with the actual question type, which is confusing.
summary.coverageAssessment: Coverage of the Oracle's 22 relationship types is narrow. The 125 questions cover approximately 10 relationship types: antonyms (Q21-32), part-whole (Q5-8, Q33-42, Q108, Q112, Q114-115, Q119), tool-user/object-function (Q1, Q4, Q13-14, Q53-62, Q71-76, Q107, Q111, Q116), young-adult (Q3, Q77-82, Q103, Q123), category-member (Q43-52, Q109, Q121), cause-effect (Q10, Q93-96, Q113), degree/intensity (Q63-70), material-product (Q20, Q83-87), animal-home (Q9, Q18, Q117), male-female (Q15, Q118), place-activity (Q88-92), synonyms (Q97-100), object-characteristic (Q101, Q106), and creator-creation (Q105, Q125). Missing entirely: country associations, collective nouns (only Q9 and Q109), sequence/order, container-contents, symbol/representation, grammatical form, action-object. Several of these are GL-tested types.
summary.teachingEfficacyAssessment: 8 lessons across 8 sub-concepts cover the most important relationship types well: Young-Adult, Tool-User, Part-Whole, Cause-Effect, Synonym-Antonym Pairs, Animal-Home, Category-Example, and Creator-Creation. Each lesson follows a solid hook-teach-interact-consolidate structure with AnalogyDisplay and WorkedExample visuals. The consolidation screens provide useful quick-reference checklists. Teaching efficacy is undermined by two issues: (1) the 'Synonym & Antonym Pairs' lesson is a catch-all bucket receiving 30 questions including degree/intensity, male-female, object-characteristic, and place-activity questions that don't match its teaching content at all, and (2) the 'Tool to User' lesson describes tool-to-person relationships but receives questions about tool-to-function (Q53-62) and object-to-action (Q107) which are different relationship types. All 125/125 questions are mapped with high confidence, but many mappings are semantically incorrect.
topPriorities: Fix duplicate questions Q78/Q103 and Q11/Q122 and Q34/Q112 and Q80/Q123 — identical answer pairs waste question slots
Fix Q35 (key is NOT part of a lock — it operates a lock) and Q105 (actor does NOT create a film — they perform in it)
Reclassify or create new sub-concepts for degree/intensity (Q63-70), place-activity (Q88-92), and male-female (Q15, Q118) — these are currently dumped into wrong lessons
Remove or replace tips that are irrelevant to the question type (e.g. degree relationship tips on part-whole questions)
Add questions for missing GL relationship types: collective nouns, country associations, sequence/order

issues.title: Q78 and Q103 are identical questions with identical answers
issues.description: Both questions ask '___ is to cow as lamb is to ___' with the answer 'calf + sheep'. Q78 (D1) and Q103 (D1) are exact duplicates — same question text, same answer, same difficulty. The only difference is one distractor word (bull vs barn in setA). This wastes a question slot in a 125-question bank.
issues.suggestedFix: Delete Q103 and replace with a different young-adult pair at D1. Consider 'piglet + pig', 'duckling + duck', or 'kid + goat'.

issues.title: Q11 and Q122 test identical answer pair 'conductor + team'
issues.description: Q11 (D3) asks '___ is to orchestra as captain is to ___' with answer 'conductor + team'. Q122 (D3) asks the identical question '___ is to orchestra as captain is to ___' with the same answer. These are exact duplicates at the same difficulty level. Different distractor words (musician/violin/ship/army vs violin/concert/ship/badge) but the core question and answer are identical.
issues.suggestedFix: Delete Q122 and replace with a different leader-to-group pair at D3. Consider 'headteacher + school', 'principal + institution', or 'admiral + fleet'.

issues.title: Q34 and Q112 test identical answer pair 'leaf + flower'
issues.description: Q34 (D1) and Q112 (D2) both ask '___ is to tree as petal is to ___' with the answer 'leaf + flower'. The question text is identical. The only difference is the distractor words in the sets and the difficulty rating (D1 vs D2). Having the same question text and answer twice is wasteful.
issues.suggestedFix: Delete Q112 and replace with a different part-whole pair at D2. Consider 'pixel + screen', 'carriage + train' (but Q42 already uses this), or 'stanza + poem'.

issues.title: Q80 and Q123 test the same caterpillar/butterfly + tadpole/frog pairing
issues.description: Q80 (D2) asks '___ is to frog as caterpillar is to ___' (tadpole + butterfly). Q123 (D3) asks '___ is to caterpillar as frog is to ___' (butterfly + tadpole). While the direction is reversed, these test identical knowledge — a child who knows the metamorphosis pairs will get both right or both wrong. The reversal does not genuinely increase difficulty.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace Q123 with a different young-adult pair at D3. Consider 'leveret + hare', 'kit + fox', or 'squab + pigeon' — these use less common baby animal names appropriate for D3.

issues.title: Q35: a key is NOT part of a lock — it operates a lock
issues.description: Q35 asks '___ is to clock as key is to ___' with answer 'hand + lock'. The explanation says 'A hand is part of a clock, just as a key is part of a lock.' The first pair is correct (a hand IS part of a clock), but a key is NOT part of a lock — it is a separate object that operates/opens a lock. This is a tool-to-object relationship, not part-to-whole. A child who correctly understands part-whole would be confused because the two pairs use different relationship types, making the analogy structurally flawed.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the answer to maintain a consistent part-whole relationship. Options: (1) Change setB to include 'lock' as a distractor and 'keyboard' as the answer (a key is part of a keyboard), which matches 'hand is part of a clock'. (2) Keep 'lock' but change the explanation to acknowledge it's a tool-function relationship, and reclassify both pairs. Option 1 is cleaner.

issues.title: Q105: an actor does not 'create' a film — they perform in it
issues.description: Q105 asks '___ is to book as actor is to ___' with answer 'author + film'. The explanation says 'An author creates a book, just as an actor performs in a film. Both are creators in their field.' This is contradictory — the explanation itself admits the relationship is 'creates' vs 'performs in', which are different relationships. An author creates a book (creator-creation), but an actor performs in a film (performer-medium). This is mapped to 'Creator to Creation' which only matches the first pair.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with a genuine creator-creation pair. Change to '___ is to book as director is to ___' (author + film) since a director creates a film. Or change to '___ is to book as composer is to ___' (author + symphony).

issues.title: Q56: a telescope magnifies, but it primarily allows viewing distant objects — 'observe' is equally valid
issues.description: Q56 asks '___ is to measure as telescope is to ___' with answer 'ruler + magnify'. While a telescope does magnify, its primary function is to observe distant objects. The word 'magnify' more precisely describes a microscope's function. The explanation says 'a telescope is used to magnify' but most would say 'a telescope is used to observe' or 'to view distant objects'. Note that Q62 already uses 'microscope + enlarge' which is the proper magnification pairing.
issues.suggestedFix: Change setB to include 'observe' instead of 'magnify', and update the answer to 'ruler + observe'. This creates a cleaner parallel: a ruler is a tool used to measure, a telescope is a tool used to observe.

issues.title: Q12 (bark/dog :: meow/cat) mapped to Part-Whole but is actually object-characteristic/animal-sound
issues.description: Q12 asks '___ is to dog as meow is to ___' (bark + cat). This is an animal-to-sound relationship: a dog makes a bark, a cat makes a meow. It is mapped to 'Part to Whole' but bark is not PART of a dog — it is the SOUND a dog makes. The lesson teaches 'a page is part of a book' which is not the same relationship type at all.
issues.suggestedFix: Remap to a more appropriate sub-concept. If no animal-sound sub-concept exists, create one or map to the closest existing concept. Alternatively, map to 'Category to Example' or create a dedicated 'Object to Characteristic' lesson.

issues.title: Q2 (glove/hand :: helmet/head) mapped to Part-Whole but is actually object-function (protection)
issues.description: Q2 asks '___ is to hand as helmet is to ___' (glove + head). The explanation says 'A glove covers and protects a hand, just as a helmet covers and protects a head.' This is an object-to-function/protection relationship, not part-whole. A glove is not PART of a hand. The Part-Whole lesson teaches 'a page is part of a book' which is a completely different relationship.
issues.suggestedFix: Remap to 'Tool to User' (closest existing match, as these are protective items matched to what they protect) or create a new sub-concept for 'Object to Function' relationships.

issues.title: Male-female pairs (Q15, Q118) mapped to Synonym & Antonym Pairs — no matching lesson exists
issues.description: Q15 (nephew/uncle :: niece/aunt) and Q118 (queen/king :: duchess/duke) are male-to-female equivalent relationships. They are mapped to 'Synonym & Antonym Pairs' which teaches about words meaning the same or opposite. But nephew is not a synonym or antonym of uncle — it's the male counterpart relationship. The lesson content about hot/cold opposites does not prepare the child for this relationship type.
issues.suggestedFix: Either create a dedicated 'Male to Female' sub-concept (it's one of the Oracle's 22 listed relationship types), or acknowledge in the Synonym-Antonym lesson that gender pairs follow a similar pattern. The Oracle explicitly lists 'Male-Female' as relationship type #11.

issues.title: Degree/intensity questions (Q63-Q70) mapped to Synonym & Antonym Pairs — lesson doesn't teach this
issues.description: Eight questions testing degree/intensity relationships (warm/boiling, whisper/shout, annoyed/furious, hungry/starving, drizzle/downpour, nervous/terrified, old/ancient, flurry/blizzard) are all mapped to 'Synonym & Antonym Pairs'. The lesson teaches about synonyms and antonyms (same/opposite meaning) but degree/intensity is a different relationship type — it's about matching the SCALE of increase, not identifying synonyms or antonyms. The Oracle lists 'Degree/intensity (mild to extreme)' as relationship type #3, distinct from synonyms (#1) and antonyms (#2).
issues.suggestedFix: Create a dedicated 'Degree & Intensity' sub-concept and lesson. This is a common GL relationship type that deserves its own teaching — children need to understand matching the jump in intensity (mild→extreme must pair with mild→extreme, not mild→moderate).

issues.title: Place-activity questions (Q88-Q92) mapped to Category to Example — not the best fit
issues.description: Q88 (school/learn), Q89 (pool/swim), Q90 (church/worship), Q91 (theatre/act), Q92 (laboratory/experiment) are place-to-activity relationships. They are mapped to 'Category to Example' which teaches 'rose is a type of flower'. A school is not a TYPE of learning — it is a PLACE where learning happens. While the mapping works at a stretch, the lesson content doesn't teach this relationship.
issues.suggestedFix: Consider creating a 'Place to Activity' sub-concept, or expand the Category to Example lesson to mention place-activity as a variant pattern.

issues.title: Leader-to-group questions mapped to 'Tool to User' — misleading lesson alignment
issues.description: Q4 (doctor/patient :: teacher/pupil) and Q11/Q122 (conductor/orchestra :: captain/team) are leader/professional-to-group relationships. They are mapped to 'Tool to User' which teaches 'a writer uses a pen'. A doctor is not a TOOL, and a conductor is not a TOOL. The lesson says 'This pattern links a tool or instrument to the person who uses it' — but these questions link a professional to who they serve/lead. A child studying the Tool-User lesson would not learn to solve these questions.
issues.suggestedFix: Create a 'Professional to Domain' or 'Leader to Group' sub-concept, or expand the Tool-User lesson to explicitly cover professional-to-role relationships alongside tool-to-user ones.

issues.title: Tip about degree relationships appears on questions that have nothing to do with degree/intensity
issues.description: The tip 'For degree relationships (warm to hot), match the INTENSITY jump' appears on 11 questions including part-whole (Q8: wheel/car, Q40: bone/skeleton), young-adult (Q80: tadpole/frog), cause-effect (Q96: moisture/rust), and tool-user (Q72: photographer/camera) questions. These tips cycle through a fixed rotation regardless of question type, which is confusing — a child doing a part-whole question is told to think about intensity jumps.
issues.suggestedFix: Match tips to question relationship types. Part-whole questions should have part-whole tips, cause-effect should have cause-effect tips, etc. At minimum, make the tips relevant to the broad category or use a generic analogy-solving tip.

issues.title: Q50: 'raptor' is an unusual category term — 'bird of prey' is the standard GL-level vocabulary
issues.description: Q50 asks '___ is to reptile as eagle is to ___' with answer 'snake + raptor'. While 'raptor' is technically correct (it means bird of prey), this is uncommon vocabulary for a 9-10 year old and not standard GL terminology. Most children would expect 'bird' as the category for eagle (which is the more direct parallel to 'snake is to reptile'). The word 'raptor' may be associated with dinosaurs (Jurassic Park) more than birds for this age group. 'Bird' is the equivalent level of category to 'reptile'.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the answer to 'snake + bird' and update setB to include 'bird' instead of or alongside 'raptor'. This creates a cleaner parallel: snake is a type of reptile, eagle is a type of bird. Both are member-to-category at the same hierarchical level.

issues.title: Q51: 'leafy vegetable' as a set option is a two-word phrase — inconsistent with single-word format
issues.description: Q51 has 'leafy vegetable' as an option in setB. All other options across all 125 questions are single words. A two-word phrase stands out and effectively gives away the answer because it's the only option that functions as a category label. The pick-from-sets format works best with single-word options.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace 'leafy vegetable' with 'vegetable' (simpler, single word, maintains the same relationship). Update the explanation accordingly.

issues.title: Q74: 'multimeter' is specialist vocabulary unlikely to be known by most 9-10 year olds
issues.description: Q74 asks '___ is to microscope as electrician is to ___' with answer 'scientist + multimeter'. While 'multimeter' is the correct specialist tool, it is very unlikely to be in the vocabulary of a 9-10 year old, even at D2 difficulty. Most GL questions at D2 use words children would have encountered through reading or school. This is rated D2 but the vocabulary is arguably D3 or beyond.
issues.suggestedFix: Either upgrade to D3 difficulty, or replace with a more recognisable tool. Consider 'electrician + wire stripper' (still specialist but more intuitive) or change the profession entirely: '___ is to microscope as builder is to ___' (scientist + hammer).

issues.title: Several GL-tested relationship types have zero or minimal coverage
issues.description: The Oracle lists 22 relationship types for verbal analogies. Of these, the following have zero questions: country associations (e.g. France/French, Japan/yen), container-contents (e.g. wallet/money, vase/flowers), symbol/representation (e.g. dove/peace, crown/royalty), sequence/order (e.g. Monday/Tuesday, spring/summer), grammatical form changes (e.g. run/running, mouse/mice). Collective nouns have only 2 questions (Q9: flock/sheep, Q109: bird/flock). These are all types that appear in GL papers and the gap means children won't practise them.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 5-10 questions per missing relationship type, distributed across D1-D3. Priority order: (1) collective nouns (very common in GL), (2) country associations, (3) container-contents, (4) sequence/order. This would require expanding from 125 to ~145-155 questions.
